checks.a_analysis: I carefully examined each slide/section in the output JSON and compared it to the corresponding page in the source PDF. No fabricated paragraphs, sentences, or section headings were found. All text in the output has a clear basis in the source material. Expected AI-generated content (titles, alt text) was not considered a defect as per the instructions.

I verified the contact information on the final page (Page 60) of the source PDF against the output (Section 59). The web address (www.ablr360.com), phone number (833-294-2257), and email (kim.casey@ablr360.com) match exactly. No other contact information was found in the document to check.

The source PDF contains 60 pages, and the output JSON contains 60 sections corresponding to each slide/page. I confirmed that all major sections, titles, and content from every page are represented in the converted output.

There are two instances where tables in the output have significantly fewer rows than in the source PDF. In Section 30 (Page 30), the 'Consent Agenda (Attachment D)' table contains 13 data rows in the source, but only 1 data row in the output. In Section 8 (Page 8), the source contains a complex table (tax form schedules), but the output generated a table shell with headers ('Form Section', 'Details') and no data rows at all. Both cases represent a loss of more than 20% of the data rows.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 30 contains a table 'Consent Agenda (Attachment D)' with 13 data rows (Charlotte through Lumberton). Page 8 contains a complex table/form with data for Schedules D, E, F, G, H, and I.
checks.b_evidence_output: Section 30: Table 'Consent Agenda (Attachment D)' contains only 1 data row ('Charlotte'). Section 8: Table contains only header row ('Form Section', 'Details') and no data rows.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: In Section 15 (Page 15), a substantial block of text within an image is missing from the output. The text 'Got your REAL ID yet? Beginning May 7, 2025, a REAL ID, U.S. passport or another federally approved identification will be required to board commercial flights and enter secure federal buildings.' constitutes a significant portion (approximately 40%) of the total informational content of that slide. This text is neither extracted into the body nor represented in the alt text for the image.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 15 text in image: 'Got your REAL ID yet? Beginning May 7, 2025, a REAL ID, U.S. passport or another federally approved identification will be required to board commercial flights and enter secure federal buildings.'
checks.b_evidence_output: Section 15 omits this text from the body and the alt text ('North Carolina Driver License example showing a real ID star.').

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: I compared the text in several sections (Agenda, Tips, Checklists) and found no wording changes that alter the meaning. The text content was preserved with high fidelity, except for the omissions noted in C4 and C5.

checks.a_analysis: I examined the JSON output slide by slide against the source PDF. No text blocks or elements appear to be duplicated. Each item from the source is represented once in the corresponding section of the output.

The JSON output uses a flat hierarchy consisting entirely of H2 tags for slide titles and sub-headings within slides. While this doesn't capture the internal nesting of some slides (e.g., Slide 15 where 'Discussion' is a sub-heading), it is consistent and avoids level skipping (e.g., H1 to H3). This normalization is explicitly permitted in the 'Acceptable Changes' section of the instructions.

The tables on Slide 8 and Slide 30 are correctly identified as tables. While the data within them is truncated (representing only a portion of the source data), the structural components (header rows and data rows) are properly defined. Data completeness is outside the scope of this structural audit.

The reading order in the JSON output follows the slide sequence of the source PDF. Within each slide, the elements are ordered logically, following the top-to-bottom and left-to-right flow of the source. On Slide 18, the grouping of the two text blocks correctly reflects the source's visual layout.

All lists identified in the source PDF (e.g., Agenda on Slide 3, Tips on Slide 5, common issues on Slide 13) are correctly converted into structured list elements (ordered or unordered) in the JSON. Nesting and itemization match the source material.

I verified the links in the output against the source. All URLs and contact links (e.g., Slide 43, 59) found in the PDF are correctly represented in the JSON without any broken, missing, or fabricated links.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output successfully replicates the linear, slide-based structure of the source PDF. Content is grouped by slide headings, and the flow of information matches the source presentation. While the font styling and background colors differ, the visual organization of the content blocks is consistent with the original document's intent.

Multiple tables present in the source PDF are missing or incomplete in the HTML output. Specifically, Slide 29 in the source contains three sample tables illustrating table types, none of which appear in the HTML under the corresponding heading. Furthermore, the large 'Consent Agenda' table on Slide 30 is truncated in the HTML; it missing the 'Miscellaneous Action Items' sub-section and the 'End Consent Agenda' footer row.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 29 shows three tables: 'Individual Discussion Item', 'UAL Units', and 'Weekdays/Weekend'. Page 30 shows a comprehensive 'Consent Agenda' table including a section for 'Miscellaneous Action Items'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML rendering for 'Slide 29: Types of Tables' contains only text and no tables. The rendering for 'Slide 30: Data Tables' includes only the first 'Consent Agenda' table, omitting the secondary data section and the closing row.

checks.a_analysis: A significant majority of meaningful images from the source PDF are missing from the HTML rendering. This includes portrait photos, instructional screenshots of software interfaces (Word, PowerPoint, Adobe Acrobat), diagrams (like the 'Awesome' graphic on slide 36), and illustrative photos (like the garage sorting on slide 14 or the thinking woman on slide 53). Only the company logo and one of the two portraits on Slide 2 were successfully rendered.
checks.b_evidence_source: Slide 2 has two portraits. Slide 7 has photos of assistive keyboards. Slide 14 has BEFORE/AFTER garage photos. Slide 24 has donut charts. Slide 36 has an 'Accessibility + Usability = Awesome' diagram. Slide 53 has a photo of a woman thinking.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML output is missing the second portrait on Slide 2, and all meaningful images on slides 7, 14, 24, 36, and 53, as well as many other slides containing UI screenshots or icons.

checks.a_analysis: There is no evidence of body content being duplicated in the HTML output. The appearance of slide titles in both the Table of Contents and as headings within the body is a standard accessibility feature for navigation and does not constitute content duplication.
